Explore And Visualize

Goal

Choose one course dataset, inspect it, tabulate a few variables, and create one visualization.

This practice is intentionally open-ended. The goal is to get comfortable opening a dataset, asking what is in it, and making a first plot.

Choose A Dataset

Uncomment one dataset path below, or replace it with another path from data/data.html.

# dataset_path <- "data/real/prams_2011_selected.csv"
# dataset_path <- "data/real/census_state_race_ethnicity_long.csv"
# dataset_path <- "data/real/flu_vaccination_age_time_clean.csv"
# dataset_path <- "data/real/nhanes_2021_2023_analysis.csv"
# dataset_path <- "data/real/places_county_core_measures_long.csv"

dataset_path <- "data/real/prams_2011_selected.csv"

Load And Inspect

dat <- read_csv(dataset_path, show_col_types = FALSE)

dat |>
  glimpse()
Rows: 1,222
Columns: 8
$ location_abbr                        <chr> "AR", "AR", "AR", "AR", "AR", "AR…
$ location                             <chr> "Arkansas", "Arkansas", "Arkansas…
$ subgroup_cat                         <chr> "Adequacy of Prenatal care", "Ade…
$ subgroup                             <chr> "ADEQUATE PNC", "INADEQUATE PNC",…
$ depression_within_3_months_birth     <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, N…
$ anxiety_within_3_months_birth        <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, N…
$ binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth <dbl> 24.6, 12.6, 22.2, 36.0, 21.5, 24.…
$ alcohol_use_within_3_months_birth    <dbl> 53.5, 28.7, 40.9, 54.1, 45.8, 49.…

Tabulate A Few Things

Pick one or two categorical variables and count the rows in each category.

For the default PRAMS dataset, these examples work.

dat |>
  count(subgroup_cat, sort = TRUE)
# A tibble: 18 × 2
   subgroup_cat                                   n
   <chr>                                      <int>
 1 Maternal Age - 18 to 44 years in groupings   130
 2 Adequacy of Prenatal care                    104
 3 Income (years 2004 and beyond)               104
 4 Maternal Age (4 Levels)                      104
 5 Maternal Race/Ethnicity                      104
 6 Maternal Age (3 Levels)                       78
 7 Maternal Education                            78
 8 Birth Weight                                  52
 9 Marital Status                                52
10 Medicaid Recipient                            52
11 Mother Hispanic                               52
12 Number of Previous Live Births                52
13 On WIC during Pregnancy                       52
14 Pregnancy Intendedness                        52
15 Smoked 3 months before Pregnancy              52
16 Smoked last 3 months of Pregnancy             52
17 Maternal Age - 18 to 44 years only            26
18 None                                          26
dat |>
  count(location_abbr, sort = TRUE)
# A tibble: 26 × 2
   location_abbr     n
   <chr>         <int>
 1 AR               47
 2 CO               47
 3 DE               47
 4 GA               47
 5 HI               47
 6 MA               47
 7 MD               47
 8 ME               47
 9 MI               47
10 MN               47
# ℹ 16 more rows

If you chose a different dataset, replace subgroup_cat and location_abbr with variables from your glimpse() output.

Make One Visualization

Start with one simple question.

For the default PRAMS dataset: how are rows distributed across subgroup categories?

plot_data <- dat |>
  count(subgroup_cat, sort = TRUE) |>
  mutate(subgroup_cat = reorder(subgroup_cat, n))

p1 <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = subgroup_cat, y = n))

p1
p2 <- p1 +
  geom_col()

p2
p3 <- p2 +
  coord_flip()

p3
p4 <- p3 +
  labs(
    title = "Rows By Subgroup Category",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of rows"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p4

Your Turn

Edit the code above or add a new code chunk below.

Try one of these:

  • choose a different dataset path
  • count a different categorical variable
  • make a histogram of a numeric variable
  • make a scatterplot with two numeric variables
  • improve the title and axis labels

Render Check

Render this notebook. Confirm that the rendered HTML includes your table output and your visualization.
